items.correct: No — a prime number has exactly two distinct factors (1 and itself); 1 has only one factor.
items.misconception: Yes — 1 has no factors except itself, which makes it prime.
items.random_wrong: Yes — 1 is the smallest prime number.
items.why: Students apply a loose 'only divisible by 1 and itself' rule and miss the precise requirement of exactly two distinct factors.

items.misconception: 0.6 is larger because it has more digits.
items.random_wrong: 3/5 is larger because fractions are always smaller-looking but bigger.
items.why: Students don't fluently convert between fractions and decimals and compare them by surface features rather than value.

items.q: Is the result of 8 x 0.4 larger or smaller than 8?
items.correct: Smaller — multiplying by a number between 0 and 1 makes the result smaller (the answer is 3.2).
items.misconception: Larger, because multiplying always makes numbers bigger.
items.random_wrong: The same as 8, because 0.4 is too small to change anything.
items.why: Students learn 'multiplication makes things bigger' from integer experience and don't update when fractional multipliers are introduced.

items.q: Using Newton-Raphson on f(x) = x² − 2 with x₀ = 1.5, what is x₁? (Give to 3 dp.)
items.why: Correct answer: 1.417. “1.500” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The trapezium rule estimates which kind of quantity?
items.why: Correct answer: a definite integral. “a derivative” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Use trapezium rule with 2 strips to estimate ∫₀² x² dx (h = 1).
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
